it will result in a deep stall in every case.
this can be absorbed by fully applying the brakes.
do not brake under any circumstances, or else the glider will stall. Instead the glider should be allowed to fly and, if necessary, the dive lightly checked.

Practical Flying_128.text: What must you do immediately after landing?
options: Immediately fold the glider and pack it into its backpack.
Immediately leave the landing area.
Immediately remove your harness and helmet.
Record the flight in your logbook.

Practical Flying_156.text: Which season is the most difficult for Chamois and Ibex, when a lot of them die?
options: After the first snowfall when the animals must acclimatise to the cold and don't find food anymore.
At the end of the winter (March - April), when the snow still covers the grass and their fat reserves are exhausted.
In the middle of winter, when any movement in the deep snow is almost impossible.
During the rutting season, when the males constantly chase the females and none get enough nourishment.

Practical Flying_11.text: Zürich-Kloten announces a QNH of 1015 hPa, Lugano a QNH of 1007. What does this signify?
options: The pressure drop between Zürich and Lugano results in a very strong north wind across the Alps with cloud congestion on the northern side and north föhn on the southern side of the Alps.
The pressure drop between Zürich and Lugano results in a very strong south wind across the Alps with cloud congestion on the southern side and south föhn on the northern side of the Alps.
There is colder, denser air in Zürich-Kloten than in Lugano. Good thermals can be expected in the north side of the Alps.

As Zürich-Kloten is at a higher altitude than Lugano, the pressure is somewhat higher. When converted to sea level pressure the difference over the alps is small and the resulting wind therefore weak.

Practical Flying_71.text: What safety measures should be taken when encountering thermal turbulence during ridge soaring?
options: Never fly below the ridge level.
Increase distance from ridges and adapt speed accordingly.
Leave the slope, as soaring in thermal turbulence carries too much risk.
Release the trimmers for a more homogeneous airflow over the glider, thereby reducing the chances of a stall.

Practical Flying_75.text: What are the possible consequences when a glider flying straight ahead is braked (on both sides) too much or too sharply?
options: The wing enters a constantly increasing spiral dive.
The airflow separates on one side (stalls) resulting in the wing diving over to that side and entering a spin.
The airflow separates from the wing, resulting in a parachutal stall, or the wing tips back into a full stall.
The glider suffers a front collapse, whereby the wingtips overtake the centre of the glider causing it to collapse together.

Practical Flying_104.text: When landing on a slope the situation does not allow you to approach along it, only up or down the slope. Which of the following is correct with zero wind?
options: It is easier to land more precisely but more difficult to land softly down the slope.
It is easier to land more precisely and softer down the slope.
It is easier to land more precisely but more difficult to land softly up the slope.
It is easier to land more precisely and softer up the slope.

Practical Flying_120.text: When landing with a headwind, the angle of approach is
options: steeper and the touchdown speed lower.
shallower and the touchdown speed higher.
steeper and the touchdown speed higher.
shallower and the touchdown speed lower.

Practical Flying_49.text: A pilot wants to fly as far as possible with a head wind of 25 km/h. What should he do?
options: Fly as fast as possible, opening trimmers if installed and using the accelerator as much as the glider and turbulence allows.
Fly the glider with light brake pressure (1 - 10% braked).
Fly the glider with approx. 20 - 30% brakes.
Fly the glider with approx. 50% brakes.

Practical Flying_149.text: What is meant by the "garland flight" of the golden eagle?
options: The eagle is defending his territory and his young.
The eagle is happy and flies playfully.
The eagle is hunting smaller birds.
The eagle is courting a mate.

Practical Flying_141.text: In which of the following situations should the reserve chute be deployed immediately without hesitation?
options: The right hand third of the wing is collapsed and caught in the lines (cravat).
After a spin the lines are twisted a few times and the glider starts to go into a spiral dive by itself.
The glider is being sucked into a cumulus cloud and is climbing at 8 m/s.
One side of the glider stalls and it goes into a spin.

Practical Flying_25.text: When taking off in a corridor in a forest, you have to be aware and ready for the possibility that
options: it is calm at ground level although at tree-top height the wind will be present, resulting in strong turbulence at that height.
when clearing the first trees there may be strong thermals due to the temperature difference between the forest and the clearing.
in the cool clearing between the sunny forests there will be back-wind close to the ground, making it difficult to clear the launch.
due to the large temperature difference between the forest and field, especially strong and frequent thermals with the corresponding turbulence will be present.

Practical Flying_27.text: At the launch site there is a 25km/h head wind, what is the best method to take off?
options: The Pilot should forward launch whenever conditions allow as this is by far the safest launch method.
The pilot positions himself on a steep slope as close to the glider's trailing edge as possible and accelerates as fast as possible, in order to energetically pull up the glider.
The pilot inflates the glider in reverse fashion on a shallow slope and takes care not be involuntarily lifted up before he is ready.
The pilot inflates the glider in reverse fashion on a steep slope so that he is lifted as soon as the glider is up.

Practical Flying_137.text: In which situation in air is the danger of collision between a delta and paraglider at its greatest, notably as neither of the pilots can see each other.
options: A delta approaches a paraglider from behind and from below.
A delta approaches a paraglider from behind and from above.
A delta approaches a paraglider from the front and from above.
A delta approaches a paraglider from the front and from below.

Practical Flying_159.text: Pilots can help to protect wild life most efficiently by
options: following agreements between the clubs and gamekeepers.
turning away immediately, when they see fleeing wildlife.
warning wild animals by shouting loudly.
flying close to the ground so that the wild animals can see them.

Practical Flying_115.text: Practical Flying From 69 Each diagram shows a standard landing approach in zero wind (dotted line) and a practical approach in accordance with conditions (solid line). Which approach should a pilot take with 10 - 15 km/h wind? 312

Practical Flying_67.text: A pilot is ascending in a thermal with a 10 knot wind from 270º. The thermal
options: deviates with increasing height towards the east.
deviates with increasing height towards the north.
deviates with increasing height towards the west.
rises vertically.

Practical Flying_114.text: Each diagram shows a standard landing approach in zero wind (dotted line) and a practical approach in accordance with conditions (solid line). Which approach should a pilot take with zero wind when he realises on the base leg that he is too high? 31

Practical Flying_17.text: Amongst other things, which of the following form part of the preparation for flying in a new site?
options: Inspect the landing field, find out about aerial obstacles, register the flight with the chief of the nearest airfield.
Register the flight with the chief of the nearest airfield, plan the flight according to the conditions, inspect the landing field.
Find out about aerial obstacles, register the flight with the chief of the nearest airfield, plan the flight according to the conditions.
Plan the flight according to the conditions, inspect the landing field, find out about air-space and aerial obstacles.

Practical Flying_147.text: When someone who is injured must be airlifted, before the helicopter arrives you should
options: mark the accident scene with the glider.
immediately leave the accident scene.
remove the injured person's helmet and harness and stow them in the backpack.
immediately stop all flying in the broad vicinity of the accident scene, remove all gliders, jackets and other loose articles from the accident scene and surroundings.

Practical Flying_3.text: Up until what altitude can a person in a resting state adapt to the different changing atmospheric conditions?
options: approx. 3,000m above sea level.
approx. 4,000m above sea level.
approx. 5,000m above sea level.
approx. 2,000m above sea level.

Practical Flying_76.text: What are the indications that a glider is in an inflated or parachutal stall?
options: No sensation of wind on face, no pressure in brakes and the underside of the glider is no longer tensioned, ie is concave between the line connection points.
The glider has a high sink rate and the brake pressure is very high.
The glider enters a constantly increasing spiral dive.
Wind in the face, speed over ground is zero and the glider just sinks.

Practical Flying_107.text: Which part of the approach is #2?
options: Zone for losing altitude.
Base leg.
Downwind leg.
Final approach.

Practical Flying_168.text: Are local wildlife and forest protection zones to be observed by paragliding pilots?
options: No. They are also not important for cross country pilots.
Yes, because failure to do so may jeopardize the flying site concerned.
Yes. Flying into these zones is illegal and will be punished.
No, these are purely voluntary agreements and non-compliance has no consequences.
Rapid alternating turns, whereby the glider occasionally exhibits a bank angle of over 90°.
Very tight turns whereby the G loading due to the centrifugal force increases markedly.
A rapid rotation of the wing about the vertical axis, whereby one half of the glider flies forwards, the other backwards.
Twisting of risers and lines provoked by rotation of glider around the vertical axis. Due to the pilot's inertia he can't rotate as fast as the glider.

Practical Flying_9.text: When the human body is exposed to an airflow for an extended period
options: its demand for oxygen increases considerably.
its demand for protein increases considerably.
its demand for vitamins increases considerably.
its demand for liquids increases considerably.

Practical Flying_135.text: In the case of landing in a tree what causes most serious injuries?
options: The pilot gets out of his harness without using a rope to secure himself, the branches break and he falls out of the tree.
The glider and pilot slide down the tree and fall to the ground.
The branches give way under the weight of the pilot and glider, and the pilot falls to the ground.
The pilot is injured by the crash into the tree.

Practical Flying_116.text: Each diagram shows a standard landing approach in zero wind (dotted line) and a practical approach in accordance with conditions (solid line). Which approach should a pilot take with 5 - 0 km/h wind? 4213

Practical Flying_13.text: From what difference in pressure between Zurich and Lugano can one expect foehn in the Alpine valleys?
options: From 8 hPa upwards.
From 2 hPa upwards.
From 16 hPa upwards.
From 4 hPa upwards.

Practical Flying_82.text: What are the indications that your wing has stalled on one side?
options: The glider becomes unstable about the longitudinal axis and begins to pendulum.
The tension on the inside brake suddenly decreases and gives no more resistance throughout the brake range.
The glider enters a spiral dive resulting in a constant, high G loading.
The glider half on the inside of the turn collapses due to the decrease in pressure inside the glider.

Practical Flying_127.text: On a final the pilot applies 80% brakes without stalling, if he maintains this much brake for a long time
options: it is highly likely that after a while the glider will stall.
the forward speed and sink rate will continuously decrease.
because of the high internal pressure the glider will fly safely and stably.
the glider speed will slowly and continuously increase.

Practical Flying_129.text: At the landing field there is a 25km/h wind. As soon as the pilot's feet touch the ground he should
options: pull the brakes as far down as possible to immediately stop the glider flying.
turn 180º toward the glider and pull on the B or C-lines to immediately stop the glider flying.
pull the A-risers down as far as possible so that glider front collapses and stops flying immediately.
pull one brake fully so the glider comes down on its leading edge and ceases to produce lift.

Practical Flying_77.text: A pilot finds himself in an inflated or parachutal stall 500m above ground. What should he do?
options: Pull one brake line abruptly fully down and immediately release again.
Pull both brakes abruptly fully down and immediately release again.
Push out on the A-risers or apply the accelerator until the glider flies normally again.
A certified glider will recover from a parachutal stall by itself. The pilot should therefore wait with brakes lightly applied until the glider recovers.

Practical Flying_108.text: Which part of the approach is #3?
options: Zone for losing altitude.
Downwind leg.
Base leg.
Final approach.

Practical Flying_41.text: Directly after taking off the pilot realizes he must brake hard on the right side in order to fly straight ahead. What could be the cause?
options: Strong side wind from the left.
He has a knot in the left suspension lines.
He has a knot in the right suspension lines.
Light side wind from the right.

Practical Flying_40.text: Why should the brakes never be released immediately after taking off?
options: Because shortly after launch the danger exists that the static pressure in the glider drops and the glider collapses.
Because due to the turbulence that is often present close to the ground the pilot needs the brakes to pull himself back into an upright position should he touch the ground again.
Because the glider needs to be strongly braked directly after launch in order to increase glider performance enough to take off.
In the event of a collapse close to ground caused by turbulence a pilot has no time for correction if he first has to search for brakes.

Practical Flying_59.text: The angle of correction for wind drift (crab angle)
options: is not influenced by the glider speed.
will become smaller when speed is increased.
will become larger when speed is increased.
is always half the angle between wind direction and trajectory.

Practical Flying_113.text: Each diagram shows a standard landing approach in zero wind (dotted line) and a practical approach in accordance with conditions (solid line). Which approach should a pilot take with zero wind when he realises on turning from downwind to base that he is too low? 1

Practical Flying_167.text: An approx. 6 x 6 metre red cloth with a diagonal yellow cross placed on the landing means
options: A practical exam for flight instructor candidates is taking place. The cross is laid out because a very precise landing is required.

This marker signals the hunt for red and white deer. Within 5km of this marker one can expect shooting noise. There is no immediate danger for paragliding pilots. The signal is purely for information.
This marker is information for approaching helicopters and denotes a helipad, (outlanding site).

This marker signals a ban on landings for aircraft, regardless of whether they are fixed wing, helicopters, paragliders or hang gliders. Respect the landing ban and use an alternative landing site. If a rescue helicopter is approaching the landing ban marker should be removed temporarily so that it doesn’t get blown away by the heli’s downwash.

Practical Flying_8.text: What happens to the human body when the core temperature is reduced below its normal range?
options: The physical performance increases and oxygen needs decrease.
The physical performance decreases and oxygen needs increase.
The physical performance and oxygen needs increase.
The physical performance and oxygen needs decrease.

Practical Flying_52.text: A pilot is flying towards his landing field exactly south of his position with a 25 km/h west wind. What should he do to arrive as high as possible?
options: He should aim the glider in a south-westerly direction and fly as fast as possible.
He should aim the glider towards the landing and fly with brakes lightly applied.
He should aim the glider in a southerly direction and fly as fast as possible.
He should aim the glider towards the landing and fly as fast as possible.

Practical Flying_95.text: When applying big ears it should be observed that
options: only the intended A-lines resp. the separate A-risers are pulled, otherwise the entire leading edge may be collapsed. [x]
answers x, y and z are correct.
with a clumsy application, the middle A-lines could be pulled as well, resulting in a full frontal collapse. [y]
inexperienced pilots can avoid the danger of a front collapse by pulling in one ear after the other. [z]

Practical Flying_65.text: A pilot is circling in a thermal with no prevailing wind. What happens when he flies too far from the centre of the thermal?
options: He ceases to climb and flies with his normal sink-rate.
The lift becomes stronger and more turbulent.
He flies into the surrounding sink and loses altitude rapidly.
The lift becomes stronger and less turbulent.

Practical Flying_134.text: Which of the following precautions should be taken when you are forced to land in still or flowing water?
options: Disconnect all electronic devices (radio, vario etc) and hold them over your head so that they don't get wet.
Tighten all harness straps so that you don't inadvertently slip out after landing and the glider sinks or floats away.
Throw the reserve just before landing so that it provides additional buoyancy in the water.
Open as many of the harness buckles as possible before landing.

Practical Flying_148.text: When does the golden eagle breed?
options: March-May
January-March
May-July
June-August

Practical Flying_83.text: While turning a pilot realizes that one side of the wing has stalled. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Pump the inside brake in order to increase the internal pressure.
Release both brakes and allow glider to regain speed.
Apply the outside brake immediately in order to stabilise the direction.
Immediately grab and hold the risers to minimise the risk of a twist.
leaves no perceptible wake turbulence when flying in the lower speed range.
leaves a band of sink behind it.
leaves a band of lift behind it.
leaves a zone of perceptible wake turbulence for a short time, which can be felt by other gliders.

Practical Flying_26.text: What indications are there that you are taking off on the leeward side of a mountain?
options: Force and direction of wind can change abruptly.
At higher altitudes the clouds drift over the launch from front.
Constant cycles between zero wind and warm front-winds of varying strength.
Constant front wind of 5 - 10 km/h.

Practical Flying_68.text: A pilot is ascending in a thermal with a 10 knot /270º wind. The thermal is deviating to the East at altitude. Which side of the thermal will the sink be at its strongest?
options: On the side facing towards the sun.
On the windward side.
On the leeward side.
On the side facing away from the sun.

Practical Flying_60.text: The size of the angle of correction for wind drift (crab angle) depends on
options: the speed of the glider, the wind direction and the wind strength.
the sink rate of the glider, the wind direction and the flight distance.
the speed of the glider, the wind direction and the flight distance.
the sink rate of the glider, the wind direction and wind strength.

Practical Flying_21.text: At launch you have a headwind of 15km/h. What can be expected in terms of distance required and speed of takeoff?
options: The take-off distance is short, the speed is low.
The take-off distance is long, the speed is high.
The take-off distance is short, the speed is high.
The take-off distance is long, the speed is low.

Practical Flying_136.text: After landing the glider is caught in the crown of a tree. Which retrieval measure will ensure that damage to the glider is minimised?
options: Undo the connection between the harness and risers.
Recover the glider using a helicopter.
Undo the quick-links on the risers and the brake line knots.
Connect a long rope to the harness and pull the glider horizontally off the tree from as far away as possible.

Practical Flying_132.text: You are forced to land with a 20km/h wind in a field surrounded by buildings. What should you expect?
options: Strong turbulence throughout the whole of the final stage of approach.
Extended float just above the ground due to the increased ground effect.
Strong turbulence at roof level, calm air at ground level.
Ideal landing conditions in a field protected from the wind.

Practical Flying_62.text: After circling for 10min in a thermal the pilot finds that his position over ground has shifted by 3km. What can we say about the force of wind?
options: It cannot be determined from the information given.
Strong wind must be anticipated at ground level.
It is 18 km/h.
It is 30 km/h.

Practical Flying_158.text: Chamois and Ibex are most bothered when pilots
options: fly over lush green mountain pastures.
fly low over the mountain forest treetops, thereby flushing out the animals lying under the trees.
fly closely along mountain slopes and cliffs above the tree line in uninhabited areas.
call out and sing in the air, thereby scaring the animals.

Practical Flying_88.text: A pilot flies through turbulent air the glider begins to pendulum a little about the longitudinal and transverse axes. What is the appropriate reaction?
options: Stabilise the pendulum using immediate and forceful opposite braking.
Brake the glider 50% and hold the risers in order to stabilise the body position somewhat.
Fly without brakes and, if need be, accelerate in order to escape the turbulence as fast as possible.
Sit relaxed in the harness and pull gently to moderately on the brakes without trying to compensate for the pendulum.
